coredata.dc:title: Feasibility study of mist cooling for lithium-ion battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: Proceedings of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy
coredata.dc:creator: Saw, Lip Huat
King, Yeong Jin
Yew, Ming Chian
Ching Ng, Tan
Chong, Wen Tong
Pambudi, Nugroho Agung
coredata.dc:description: Li-ion battery is widely used as an energy storage system in electric vehicles and it has several advantages such as long cycle life, high energy density, superior usable capacity, low self-discharge rate and fast charging capability. However, the optimum operating temperature window of the battery is limited to 25 °C - 40 °C. Actual performance can deviate substantially if it is operated at higher or lower temperatures. Although electron and ion mobility is increased at high temperature due to low internal cell resistance, It may initiate irreversible chemical reactions which can cause thermal runaway. At temperature below 0 °C, the cell’s internal resistance is high and the performance starts to deteriorate due to Lithium plating. Therefore, this highlights the importance of the thermal management system for the Li-ion battery. In this study, thermal performance of the mist cooling was studied and compared with conventional dry air cooling using computational fluid dynamic simulation. Simulation results are then validated with experimental data and the correlation between Nu and Re was derived. From this study, it is shown that mist flow rate of 0.37 gs-1 and air velocity of 2 ms-1 is sufficient to maintain the surface temperature of dummy battery below 40 °C. Hence, mist cooling can be used as a new cooling solution for the Li-ion battery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: battery temperature
CFd analysis
mist cooling
heat generation
Li-ion battery

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: rawtext articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids confeditor contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight oauserlicense openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content oa subj suppl tomb vol volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: Proceedings of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Energy Management, Policy, Economics and Sustainability

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: © 2017 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.comAvailable online at w.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling Assessing the feasibility of using the heat demand-outdoor temperature function for a long-term district heat demand forecast I. AndriÄ‡a,b,c*, A. Pinaa, P. FerrÃ£oa, J. Fournierb., B. LacarriÃ¨rec, O. Le Correc aIN+ Center for Innovation, Technology and Policy Research - Instituto Superior TÃ©cnico, Av. Rovisco Pais 1, 1049-001 Lisbon, Portugal bVeolia Recherche & Innovation, 291 Avenue Dreyfous Daniel, 78520 Limay, France cDÃ©partement SystÃ¨mes Ã‰nergÃ©tiques et Environnement - IMT Atlantique, 4 rue Alfred Kastler, 44300 Nantes, France Abstract District heating networks are commonly addressed in the literature as one of the most effective solutions for decreasing the greenhouse gas emissions from the building sector. These systems require high investments which are returned through the heat sales. Due to the changed climate conditions and building renovation policies, heat demand in the future could decrease, prolonging the investment return period. The main scope of this paper is to assess the feasibility of using the heat demand â€“ outdoor temperature function for heat demand forecast. The district of Alvalade, located in Lisbon (Portugal), was used as a case study. The district is consisted of 665 buildings that vary in both construction period and typology. Three weather scenarios (low, medium, high) and three district renovation scenarios were developed (shallow, intermediate, deep). To estimate the error, obtained heat demand values were compared with results from a dynamic heat demand model, previously developed and validated by the authors. The results showed that when only weather change is considered, the margin of error could be acceptable for some applications (the error in annual demand was lower than 20% for all weather scenarios considered). However, after introducing renovation scenarios, the error value increased up to 59.5% (depending on the weather and renovation scenarios combination considered). The value of slope coefficient increased on average within the range of 3.8% up to 8% per decade, that corresponds to the decrease in the number of heating hours of 22-139h during the heating season (depending on the combination of weather and renovation scenarios considered). On the other hand, function intercept increased for 7.8-12.7% per decade (depending on the coupled scenarios). The values suggested could be used to modify the function parameters for the scenarios considered, and improve the accuracy of heat demand estimations. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. Keywords: Heat demand; Forecast; Climate change Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 2592 2597 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy. 10.1016/j.egypro.2017.12.197 10.1016/j.egypro.2017.12.197 1876-6102 Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy. 9th International Conference on Applied Energy, ICAE2017, 21-24 August 2017, Cardiff, UK Feasibility study of mist cooling for lithium-ion battery Lip Huat Sawa,*, Yeong Jin Kinga, Ming Chian Yewa, Tan Ching Nga, Wen Tong Chongb, Nugroho Agung Pambudic aLee Kong Chian Faculty of Engineering and Science, UTAR, Kajang, 43000, Malaysia. bDepartment of Mechanical Engineering, Fauclty of Engineering, University of Malaya, Kuala Lumpur, 50603, Malaysia. cMechanical Engineering Education, Universitas Negeri Sebelas Maret, Jl. Ir. Sutami 36A, Surakarta 57126, Indonesia. Abstract Li-ion battery is widely used as an energy storage system in electric vehicles and it has several advantages such as long cycle life, high energy density, superior usable capacity, low self-discharge rate and fast charging capability. However, the optimum operating temperature window of the battery is limited to 25 oC - 40 oC. Actual performance can deviate substantially if it is operated at higher or lower temperatures. Although electron and ion mobility is increased at high temperature due to low internal cell resistance, It may initiate irreversible chemical reactions which can cause thermal runaway. At temperature below 0 oC, the cellâ€™s internal resistance is high and the performance starts to deteriorate due to Lithium plating. Therefore, this highlights the importance of the thermal management system for the Li-ion battery. In this study, thermal performance of the mist cooling was studied and compared with conventional dry air cooling using computational fluid dynamic simulation. Simulation results are then validated with experimental data and the correlation between Nu and Re was derived. From this study, it is shown that mist flow rate of 0.37 gs-1 and air velocity of 2 ms-1 is sufficient to maintain the surface temperature of dummy battery below 40 oC. Hence, mist cooling can be used as a new cooling solution for the Li-ion battery. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy. Keywords: battery temperature; CFd analysis; mist cooling; heat generation; Li-ion battery * Corresponding author. Tel.: +603-90860288; fax: +603-90193886. E-mail address: bernardsaw81@yahoo.com; sawlh@utar.edu.my 2 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 1. Introduction Advancement of science and technology have caused global warming and greenhouse effect. Automotive engineers are trying to reduce fossil fuel consumption and divert attention toward electric vehicle (EV). Electric vehicle will not emit harmful gases such as CO, CO2, NOx, HC and particulate matter that will contribute to the greenhouse effect. Although charging the EV need to use electricity generated by burning fossil fuel, the use of renewable energy such as wind energy, solar energy and hydroelectric is slowly replacing the fossil fuel as the main energy source. Li-ion battery is widely as an energy storage system in the EV. This is due to long cycle life, high energy density, low self-discharge rate, high usable capacity and fast charging capability. Compared with Nickel-Metal Hydride and lead acid battery, Li-ion battery provides three times better performance in terms of energy and power [1]. However, performance of the Li-ion battery is sensitive to the operating temperature. At high temperature, cellâ€™s internal resistance is reduced and mobility of electron and ion is improved. Thus, more instantaneous power can be extracted from the battery. However, an increase of temperature will also initiate irreversible chemical reactions and trigger thermal runaway of the battery. As the operating temperature of the Li-ion battery increased from 25â€“55 oC, the degradation rate is increased from 4.22% to 13.24%. Furthermore, cell impedance is also increased from 33.64% to 93.29% [2]. At low temperature, internal resistance is increased and capacity of the cell is reduced. Lithium plating on the anode and cathode may occur during low temperature operation. In general, optimum operating temperature of Li-ion battery is 25 oC - 40 oC [3]. This highlight the importance of the thermal management system for Li-ion battery to ensure every single cell operates at its optimum condition. There are various types of cooling system used in the EV battery pack such as air cooling, liquid cooling, phase change or a combination of them [1, 4-6]. Among cooling technologies available, air cooling has absolute advantages compared to other types of cooling system such as low manufacturing cost, simple battery pack layout and high reliability. However, air cooling still suffered from several drawbacks such as low cooling performance, maximum flow rate of cooling air is restricted to 100 m3h-1 â€“ 250 m3h-1 and inhomogeneous temperature distribution during the cooling process. Evaporative cooling has become a focus in thermal management research due to its ability of dissipating high heat fluxes (about 40kWm-2) at affordable cost [7-8]. Coolant is evaporated by absorbing a significant amount of heat from the heat source to provide a cooling effect. Compared to single phase forced convection, evaporating process which involved liquid-vapor phase change offered the highest heat transfer rate [7]. In the two phase cooling process, coolant droplets rest on the heated surface absorbed the heat and vaporized. Amount of heat that can be dissipated from the heated surface is determined from the coolant latent heat of vaporization. Besides, area coverage of the droplet will determine the degree of surface cooling. Jiang et al. investigate the cooling performance on the gas turbine vane. It is found that mist cooling can decrease the boundary layer temperature and small uniform droplet provide better heat transfer enhancement than large droplet [8]. Although the phenomenon of the spray generation and droplet to surface impact has been investigated for years such as aerosols, airbrushes and inkjet printing, the application of mist cooling on the Li-ion battery thermal management system is relatively new. In this study, a novel minimum quantity coolant of mist cooling is proposed for the thermal management system of the Li-ion battery pack. The coolant is atomized by using ultrasonic mist generator. Injected distilled water droplet is evaporated by absorbing heat from the battery surface to provide a cooling effect. Computational fluid dynamic analysis is conducted to investigate the performance of the mist cooling system. Heat transfer correlation with the Reynolds number is then obtained from the simulation results. Finally, experiments with dummy battery were conducted to validate the numerical simulation results. 2. Design and analysis of the battery module 2.1. Dummy battery test rig design The battery used in this study is a cylindrical LFP cells with 8Ah capacity (Headway) as shown in Fig. 1(a), while the design of the dummy battery test rig is shown in Fig. 1(b). The diameter of the dummy battery is 38 mm and length is 146 mm. The dummy battery is made out of a copper rod. Parameters of the cell used in this study are tabulated in Table 1. A constant flow rate of dry air and mist were supplied to the test rig to provide cooling for the dummy battery. Lip Huat Saw et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 2592â€“2597 2593 Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy. 9th International Conference on Applied Energy, ICAE2017, 21-24 August 2017, Cardiff, UK Feasibility study of mist cooling for lithium-ion battery Lip Huat Sawa,*, Yeong Jin Kinga, Ming Chian Yewa, Tan Ching Nga, Wen Tong Chongb, Nugroho Agung Pambudic aLee Kong Chian Faculty of Engineering and Science, UTAR, Kajang, 43000, Malaysia. bDepartment of Mechanical Engineering, Fauclty of Engineering, University of Malaya, Kuala Lumpur, 50603, Malaysia. cMechanical Engineering Education, Universitas Negeri Sebelas Maret, Jl. Ir. Sutami 36A, Surakarta 57126, Indonesia. Abstract Li-ion battery is widely used as an energy storage system in electric vehicles and it has several advantages such as long cycle life, high energy density, superior usable capacity, low self-discharge rate and fast charging capability. However, the optimum operating temperature window of the battery is limited to 25 oC - 40 oC. Actual performance can deviate substantially if it is operated at higher or lower temperatures. Although electron and ion mobility is increased at high temperature due to low internal cell resistance, It may initiate irreversible chemical reactions which can cause thermal runaway. At temperature below 0 oC, the cellâ€™s internal resistance is high and the performance starts to deteriorate due to Lithium plating. Therefore, this highlights the importance of the thermal management system for the Li-ion battery. In this study, thermal performance of the mist cooling was studied and compared with conventional dry air cooling using computational fluid dynamic simulation. Simulation results are then validated with experimental data and the correlation between Nu and Re was derived. From this study, it is shown that mist flow rate of 0.37 gs-1 and air velocity of 2 ms-1 is sufficient to maintain the surface temperature of dummy battery below 40 oC. Hence, mist cooling can be used as a new cooling solution for the Li-ion battery. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy. Keywords: battery temperature; CFd analysis; mist cooling; heat generation; Li-ion battery * Corresponding author. Tel.: +603-90860288; fax: +603-90193886. E-mail address: bernardsaw81@yahoo.com; sawlh@utar.edu.my 2 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 1. Introduction Advancement of science and technology have caused global warming and greenhouse effect. Automotive engineers are trying to reduce fossil fuel consumption and divert attention toward electric vehicle (EV). Electric vehicle will not emit harmful gases such as CO, CO2, NOx, HC and particulate matter that will contribute to the greenhouse effect. Although charging the EV need to use electricity generated by burning fossil fuel, the use of renewable energy such as wind energy, solar energy and hydroelectric is slowly replacing the fossil fuel as the main energy source. Li-ion battery is widely as an energy storage system in the EV. This is due to long cycle life, high energy density, low self-discharge rate, high usable capacity and fast charging capability. Compared with Nickel-Metal Hydride and lead acid battery, Li-ion battery provides three times better performance in terms of energy and power [1]. However, performance of the Li-ion battery is sensitive to the operating temperature. At high temperature, cellâ€™s internal resistance is reduced and mobility of electron and ion is improved. Thus, more instantaneous power can be extracted from the battery. However, an increase of temperature will also initiate irreversible chemical reactions and trigger thermal runaway of the battery. As the operating temperature of the Li-ion battery increased from 25â€“55 oC, the degradation rate is increased from 4.22% to 13.24%. Furthermore, cell impedance is also increased from 33.64% to 93.29% [2]. At low temperature, internal resistance is increased and capacity of the cell is reduced. Lithium plating on the anode and cathode may occur during low temperature operation. In general, optimum operating temperature of Li-ion battery is 25 oC - 40 oC [3]. This highlight the importance of the thermal management system for Li-ion battery to ensure every single cell operates at its optimum condition. There are various types of cooling system used in the EV battery pack such as air cooling, liquid cooling, phase change or a combination of them [1, 4-6]. Among cooling technologies available, air cooling has absolute advantages compared to other types of cooling system such as low manufacturing cost, simple battery pack layout and high reliability. However, air cooling still suffered from several drawbacks such as low cooling performance, maximum flow rate of cooling air is restricted to 100 m3h-1 â€“ 250 m3h-1 and inhomogeneous temperature distribution during the cooling process. Evaporative cooling has become a focus in thermal management research due to its ability of dissipating high heat fluxes (about 40kWm-2) at affordable cost [7-8]. Coolant is evaporated by absorbing a significant amount of heat from the heat source to provide a cooling effect. Compared to single phase forced convection, evaporating process which involved liquid-vapor phase change offered the highest heat transfer rate [7]. In the two phase cooling process, coolant droplets rest on the heated surface absorbed the heat and vaporized. Amount of heat that can be dissipated from the heated surface is determined from the coolant latent heat of vaporization. Besides, area coverage of the droplet will determine the degree of surface cooling. Jiang et al. investigate the cooling performance on the gas turbine vane. It is found that mist cooling can decrease the boundary layer temperature and small uniform droplet provide better heat transfer enhancement than large droplet [8]. Although the phenomenon of the spray generation and droplet to surface impact has been investigated for years such as aerosols, airbrushes and inkjet printing, the application of mist cooling on the Li-ion battery thermal management system is relatively new. In this study, a novel minimum quantity coolant of mist cooling is proposed for the thermal management system of the Li-ion battery pack. The coolant is atomized by using ultrasonic mist generator. Injected distilled water droplet is evaporated by absorbing heat from the battery surface to provide a cooling effect. Computational fluid dynamic analysis is conducted to investigate the performance of the mist cooling system. Heat transfer correlation with the Reynolds number is then obtained from the simulation results. Finally, experiments with dummy battery were conducted to validate the numerical simulation results. 2. Design and analysis of the battery module 2.1. Dummy battery test rig design The battery used in this study is a cylindrical LFP cells with 8Ah capacity (Headway) as shown in Fig. 1(a), while the design of the dummy battery test rig is shown in Fig. 1(b). The diameter of the dummy battery is 38 mm and length is 146 mm. The dummy battery is made out of a copper rod. Parameters of the cell used in this study are tabulated in Table 1. A constant flow rate of dry air and mist were supplied to the test rig to provide cooling for the dummy battery. 2594 Lip Huat Saw et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 2592â€“2597 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 3 2.2. Numerical modeling The dummy battery test rig model was constructed using Solidworks 2016 and commercial Computational Fluid Dynamic software ANSYS-CFX was used to complement the experimental study and gain an insight into the flow field and thermal performance of the dummy battery under different velocity of dry air and mist. Uniform volumetric heat source was used to model the heat generated in the cell. 5 W per cell of uniform heat generation which corresponded to maximum heat generation at 1 C-rate of discharge was applied for the CFD analysis [9]. Fig. 1 (a). 38120 LFP cell. (b) Test rig design for single cell. Table 1. LFP cell parameters [4]. Parameter Value Parameter Value Nominal voltage, V 3.2 Internal resistance, ï�— 0.0034 Nominal capacity, Ah 8.0 Cell diameter, m 0.038 Weight, kg 0.335 Cell length, m 0.146 Cathode material LiFePO4 Anode material Graphite Specific heat capacity, Jkg-1K-1 998 Reference temperature, K 303.15 The cooling air was deemed to be steady and incompressible. While mist was assumed to be uniformly distributed in the cooling air stream and enter the battery module. Different velocity of dry air and mist with turbulence intensity of 5 % is assigned to the inlet. Inlet air temperature is set to 30 oC. Pressure boundary condition is assigned to the outlet. Wall boundaries were modeled using non-slip wall boundary condition. Two equations turbulence model K- epsilon (K-ï�¥) with scalable wall function was selected to model the turbulent flow in the test rig. First order advection scheme for turbulence equations and high resolution discretization scheme were used to achieve a high accuracy results. The convergence criteria for momentum and energy equations are set to 1.0 x 10-6 for all cases. Total computational time is about 8 hours on the workstation with Intel i5-6200U, 2.40 GHz CPU and 16 GB RAM. Boundary conditions settings for the test rig are shown in Fig. 2. Table 2. Grid independent test results. Mesh No Mesh-1 Mesh-2 Relative changed Mesh-3 Relative changed No of element 3462732 5497946 7379809 Velocity, ms-1 1 1 1 Average temperature , oC 48.96 48.71 0.51 % 48.31 0.82 % Pressure drop, Pa 1.250 1.198 2.44 % 1.234 3.01 % Grid sensitivity analysis is important for CFD simulation to reduce the error due to mesh generation. Mesh of the model must be fine enough to capture the flow and thermal behavior of the object. Grid sensitivity analysis results of the test rig are tabulated in Table 2. Three different sizes of element were generated, Mesh-1 with 4919835 elements, Mesh-2 with 5497946 elements and Mesh-3 with 7379809 elements to assess the effect of the elements number on the accuracy of the numerical modeling results. Velocity of the cooling air is set to 1 ms-1. As shown in Table 2, the relative changed of pressure drop across Mesh-2 and Mesh-3 is about 3 %. While relative changed of temperature for Mesh-2 and Mesh-3 is about 0.67%. Since all relative changed of the results is less than 5 %, then, Mesh-3 with 7379809 elements is sufficient to characterize the flow field and thermal behavior of the test rig. 4 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 Fig. 2 Boundary conditions of the single cell test rig. 2.3. Experimental setup Experimental testing using same dimension of copper rod with the battery was used to validate the simulation results. Cylindrical cartridge heater with diameter of 6 mm was inserted into the centre of the copper rod to supply constant heat flux of 5 W. The dummy battery was located 0.3 m away from the elbow to ensure uniform flow and not affected by vortices generation. Fig. 3 shows the air flow direction and location of thermocouples attached to the dummy battery. Axial fan (SanAce40, Sanyo Denki) was installed at the straight end of the tube to provide different flow rate of cooling air. Ultrasonic mist generator was placed at the other end to generator different amount of mist. 440 ml/h amounts of mist were supplied by each mist generator. In this study, 3 ultrasonic mist generators were used to produce the amount of mist needed for the experimental work. Distilled water was used as a coolant. Surface temperature of the dummy battery was measured using six thermocouples (K-type) attached to different locations on the dummy battery surface. Three thermocouples were attached in the axial direction and both sides of the dummy battery. All tests were repeated three times and the average value was taken. The temperature readings were recorded using HP34970A data acquisition system, while air flow velocity was measured using hot wire thermo-anemometer (Extech instruments). Fig. 3 Schematic of the experimental setup. Lip Huat Saw et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 2592â€“2597 2595 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 3 2.2. Numerical modeling The dummy battery test rig model was constructed using Solidworks 2016 and commercial Computational Fluid Dynamic software ANSYS-CFX was used to complement the experimental study and gain an insight into the flow field and thermal performance of the dummy battery under different velocity of dry air and mist. Uniform volumetric heat source was used to model the heat generated in the cell. 5 W per cell of uniform heat generation which corresponded to maximum heat generation at 1 C-rate of discharge was applied for the CFD analysis [9]. Fig. 1 (a). 38120 LFP cell. (b) Test rig design for single cell. Table 1. LFP cell parameters [4]. Parameter Value Parameter Value Nominal voltage, V 3.2 Internal resistance, ï�— 0.0034 Nominal capacity, Ah 8.0 Cell diameter, m 0.038 Weight, kg 0.335 Cell length, m 0.146 Cathode material LiFePO4 Anode material Graphite Specific heat capacity, Jkg-1K-1 998 Reference temperature, K 303.15 The cooling air was deemed to be steady and incompressible. While mist was assumed to be uniformly distributed in the cooling air stream and enter the battery module. Different velocity of dry air and mist with turbulence intensity of 5 % is assigned to the inlet. Inlet air temperature is set to 30 oC. Pressure boundary condition is assigned to the outlet. Wall boundaries were modeled using non-slip wall boundary condition. Two equations turbulence model K- epsilon (K-ï�¥) with scalable wall function was selected to model the turbulent flow in the test rig. First order advection scheme for turbulence equations and high resolution discretization scheme were used to achieve a high accuracy results. The convergence criteria for momentum and energy equations are set to 1.0 x 10-6 for all cases. Total computational time is about 8 hours on the workstation with Intel i5-6200U, 2.40 GHz CPU and 16 GB RAM. Boundary conditions settings for the test rig are shown in Fig. 2. Table 2. Grid independent test results. Mesh No Mesh-1 Mesh-2 Relative changed Mesh-3 Relative changed No of element 3462732 5497946 7379809 Velocity, ms-1 1 1 1 Average temperature , oC 48.96 48.71 0.51 % 48.31 0.82 % Pressure drop, Pa 1.250 1.198 2.44 % 1.234 3.01 % Grid sensitivity analysis is important for CFD simulation to reduce the error due to mesh generation. Mesh of the model must be fine enough to capture the flow and thermal behavior of the object. Grid sensitivity analysis results of the test rig are tabulated in Table 2. Three different sizes of element were generated, Mesh-1 with 4919835 elements, Mesh-2 with 5497946 elements and Mesh-3 with 7379809 elements to assess the effect of the elements number on the accuracy of the numerical modeling results. Velocity of the cooling air is set to 1 ms-1. As shown in Table 2, the relative changed of pressure drop across Mesh-2 and Mesh-3 is about 3 %. While relative changed of temperature for Mesh-2 and Mesh-3 is about 0.67%. Since all relative changed of the results is less than 5 %, then, Mesh-3 with 7379809 elements is sufficient to characterize the flow field and thermal behavior of the test rig. 4 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 Fig. 2 Boundary conditions of the single cell test rig. 2.3. Experimental setup Experimental testing using same dimension of copper rod with the battery was used to validate the simulation results. Cylindrical cartridge heater with diameter of 6 mm was inserted into the centre of the copper rod to supply constant heat flux of 5 W. The dummy battery was located 0.3 m away from the elbow to ensure uniform flow and not affected by vortices generation. Fig. 3 shows the air flow direction and location of thermocouples attached to the dummy battery. Axial fan (SanAce40, Sanyo Denki) was installed at the straight end of the tube to provide different flow rate of cooling air. Ultrasonic mist generator was placed at the other end to generator different amount of mist. 440 ml/h amounts of mist were supplied by each mist generator. In this study, 3 ultrasonic mist generators were used to produce the amount of mist needed for the experimental work. Distilled water was used as a coolant. Surface temperature of the dummy battery was measured using six thermocouples (K-type) attached to different locations on the dummy battery surface. Three thermocouples were attached in the axial direction and both sides of the dummy battery. All tests were repeated three times and the average value was taken. The temperature readings were recorded using HP34970A data acquisition system, while air flow velocity was measured using hot wire thermo-anemometer (Extech instruments). Fig. 3 Schematic of the experimental setup. 2596 Lip Huat Saw et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 2592â€“2597 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 5 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Forced air convection Forced air cooling is always a preferred choice for thermal management system in the Li-ion battery pack. Maximum temperature and variation of temperature are important factors in the thermal management system design. Variation of temperature must be less than 5 oC to ensure even charging and discharging of the cells in the battery pack. Simulation and experimental results of the average dummy battery surface temperature under different velocity of dry air are illustrated in Fig. 4. Average surface temperature of the dummy battery is reduced with the increased of the dry air velocity. Average temperature of the dummy battery is reduced to below 40 oC under 3 ms-1 of dry air. Moreover, there is about 5 % difference in the simulation and experimental results. The temperature distribution across the dummy battery is uniformly distributed. Variation of temperature of the simulation results is less than 0.5 oC. While comparing the simulation and experimental data, there is less than 1 oC of temperature variation difference. Correlation of Nu with Re can be expressed as in equation below. Nu = 0.135Re0.683 (1) Fig. 4 Change of dummy battery surface temperature with different velocity of dry air. 3.2. Mist cooling Next, performance of mist cooling is compared with forced air cooling. Mist cooling is a cooling technique used fine water droplets injected into the air stream to cool the heated surface. The size of the droplet is less than 5 microns. Mist have higher specific heat capacity than dry air and does not wet the cooling surface. Specific heat capacity of air is 1003.5 Jkg-1K-1, while specific heat capacity of distilled water is 4181.3 Jkg-1K-1. Compared to forced air cooling, higher specific heat capacity of the mist enables the air stream to maintain the cooling capacity consistently from upstream to downstream. Hence, mist cooling is a suitable thermal management solution for Li-ion battery with considering the cooling effect and cost. Thermal performance of the mist cooling with a constant mass flow rate of 0.37 gs-1 of mist is illustrated in Fig. 5. About 2 ms-1 of dry air and 0.37 gs-1 of mist is sufficient to maintain the dummy battery surface temperature below 40 oC. There is about 2.2 oC difference in the simulation and experimental results for 1 ms-1 of dry air flow. As shown in Fig. 5, experimental result for air velocity of 3 ms-1 is lower than the simulation result. This is due to the accumulation of mist to form a large droplet and condensate on the surface of the dummy battery. Eventually this will cause lower temperature recorded on the dummy battery surface and this phenomenon is not modelled in the simulation. The variation of the maximum and minimum temperature of the dummy battery is about 2.3 oC. Correlation of Nu with Re for mist cooling can be expressed as in equation below. 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 Nu = 0.8368Re0.5012 (2) Fig. 5. Change of dummy battery surface temperature with different velocity of mist. 4. Conclusions Computational fluid dynamics analysis was used to analyses the cooling performance of the dummy battery under forced air convection and mist cooling. Experimental work using ultrasonic mist generator to supply a mist into the air stream was conducted to validate the simulation results. Simulation results showed that 3 ms-1 of dry air is sufficient to maintain the dummy battery temperature well below 40 oC. On the other hand, when mist is added into the air stream to enhance the poor heat transfer performance of dry air, It is observed that by adding 0.37 gs-1 of mist into 2 ms-1 of cooling air is adequate to reduce the dummy battery surface temperature below 40 oC. The coolant is evaporated by absorbing a significant amount of heat from the heated surface and provide a cooling effect. Through injection of mist into the dry air stream, heat transfer performance of the cooling air can be extended further to the downstream. Through this study, it is concluded that mist cooling could provide an alternative cooling solution for Li-ion battery pack. In future study, mist cooling on a battery module will be investigated numerically and experimentally to assess the effect of mist cooling for a battery pack using different droplet size. Acknowledgements This work was supported by UTARRF Grant No. IPSR/RMC/UTARRF/2016-C1/B2 from UTAR and Science fund Grant No. 03-02-11-SF2016 from Ministry of Science, Technology and innovation, Malaysia. References [1] Rao Z, Wang S. A review of power battery thermal energy management. Rew Sust Energ Rev 2011;15:4554-4571. [2] Leng F, Tan, CM, Pecht M. Effect of temperature on the aging rate of Li-ion battery operating above room temperature. Sci. Rep 2015;5:12967. [3] Saw LH, Tay AAO, Zhang WL. Thermal management of Lithium-ion battery pack with liquid cooling. 31st SEMI-THERM 2015;298-302. [4] Saw LH, Ye Y, Tay AAO, Chong WT, Kuan SH, Yew MC. Computational fluid dynamic and thermal analysis of Lithium-ion battery pack with air cooling. Appl Energ 2016;177:783-792. [5] Jarrett A, Kim IY. Influence of operating conditions on the optimum design of electric vehicle battery cooling plates. J Pow Sour 2014;245:644- 655. [6] Somasundaram K, Birgersson E, Mujumdar AS. Thermal-electrochemical model for passive thermal management of a spiral-wound lithium- ion battery. J Pow Sour 2012;203:84-96. [7] Howes JC, Levett DB, Wilson ST, Marsala J, Saums DL. Cooling of an IGBT drive system with vaporizable dielectric fluid (VDF). 24th IEEE SEMI-THERM symposium. [8] Jiang Y, Zheng Q, Dong P, Yao J, Zhang H, Gao J. Conjugate heat transfer analysis of leading edge and downstream mist-air film cooling on turbine vane. Int J Heat Mass Trans 2015;90:613-626. [9] Saw LH, Ye Y, Tay AAO. Electro-thermal analysis and integration issues of Lithium-ion battery for electric vehicles. Appl Energ 2014;97- 107. Lip Huat Saw et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 2592â€“2597 2597 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 5 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Forced air convection Forced air cooling is always a preferred choice for thermal management system in the Li-ion battery pack. Maximum temperature and variation of temperature are important factors in the thermal management system design. Variation of temperature must be less than 5 oC to ensure even charging and discharging of the cells in the battery pack. Simulation and experimental results of the average dummy battery surface temperature under different velocity of dry air are illustrated in Fig. 4. Average surface temperature of the dummy battery is reduced with the increased of the dry air velocity. Average temperature of the dummy battery is reduced to below 40 oC under 3 ms-1 of dry air. Moreover, there is about 5 % difference in the simulation and experimental results. The temperature distribution across the dummy battery is uniformly distributed. Variation of temperature of the simulation results is less than 0.5 oC. While comparing the simulation and experimental data, there is less than 1 oC of temperature variation difference. Correlation of Nu with Re can be expressed as in equation below. Nu = 0.135Re0.683 (1) Fig. 4 Change of dummy battery surface temperature with different velocity of dry air. 3.2. Mist cooling Next, performance of mist cooling is compared with forced air cooling. Mist cooling is a cooling technique used fine water droplets injected into the air stream to cool the heated surface. The size of the droplet is less than 5 microns. Mist have higher specific heat capacity than dry air and does not wet the cooling surface. Specific heat capacity of air is 1003.5 Jkg-1K-1, while specific heat capacity of distilled water is 4181.3 Jkg-1K-1. Compared to forced air cooling, higher specific heat capacity of the mist enables the air stream to maintain the cooling capacity consistently from upstream to downstream. Hence, mist cooling is a suitable thermal management solution for Li-ion battery with considering the cooling effect and cost. Thermal performance of the mist cooling with a constant mass flow rate of 0.37 gs-1 of mist is illustrated in Fig. 5. About 2 ms-1 of dry air and 0.37 gs-1 of mist is sufficient to maintain the dummy battery surface temperature below 40 oC. There is about 2.2 oC difference in the simulation and experimental results for 1 ms-1 of dry air flow. As shown in Fig. 5, experimental result for air velocity of 3 ms-1 is lower than the simulation result. This is due to the accumulation of mist to form a large droplet and condensate on the surface of the dummy battery. Eventually this will cause lower temperature recorded on the dummy battery surface and this phenomenon is not modelled in the simulation. The variation of the maximum and minimum temperature of the dummy battery is about 2.3 oC. Correlation of Nu with Re for mist cooling can be expressed as in equation below. 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 Nu = 0.8368Re0.5012 (2) Fig. 5. Change of dummy battery surface temperature with different velocity of mist. 4. Conclusions Computational fluid dynamics analysis was used to analyses the cooling performance of the dummy battery under forced air convection and mist cooling. Experimental work using ultrasonic mist generator to supply a mist into the air stream was conducted to validate the simulation results. Simulation results showed that 3 ms-1 of dry air is sufficient to maintain the dummy battery temperature well below 40 oC. On the other hand, when mist is added into the air stream to enhance the poor heat transfer performance of dry air, It is observed that by adding 0.37 gs-1 of mist into 2 ms-1 of cooling air is adequate to reduce the dummy battery surface temperature below 40 oC. The coolant is evaporated by absorbing a significant amount of heat from the heated surface and provide a cooling effect. Through injection of mist into the dry air stream, heat transfer performance of the cooling air can be extended further to the downstream. Through this study, it is concluded that mist cooling could provide an alternative cooling solution for Li-ion battery pack. In future study, mist cooling on a battery module will be investigated numerically and experimentally to assess the effect of mist cooling for a battery pack using different droplet size. Acknowledgements This work was supported by UTARRF Grant No. IPSR/RMC/UTARRF/2016-C1/B2 from UTAR and Science fund Grant No. 03-02-11-SF2016 from Ministry of Science, Technology and innovation, Malaysia. References [1] Rao Z, Wang S. A review of power battery thermal energy management. Rew Sust Energ Rev 2011;15:4554-4571. [2] Leng F, Tan, CM, Pecht M. Effect of temperature on the aging rate of Li-ion battery operating above room temperature. Sci. Rep 2015;5:12967. [3] Saw LH, Tay AAO, Zhang WL. Thermal management of Lithium-ion battery pack with liquid cooling. 31st SEMI-THERM 2015;298-302. [4] Saw LH, Ye Y, Tay AAO, Chong WT, Kuan SH, Yew MC. Computational fluid dynamic and thermal analysis of Lithium-ion battery pack with air cooling. Appl Energ 2016;177:783-792. [5] Jarrett A, Kim IY. Influence of operating conditions on the optimum design of electric vehicle battery cooling plates. J Pow Sour 2014;245:644- 655. [6] Somasundaram K, Birgersson E, Mujumdar AS. Thermal-electrochemical model for passive thermal management of a spiral-wound lithium- ion battery. J Pow Sour 2012;203:84-96. [7] Howes JC, Levett DB, Wilson ST, Marsala J, Saums DL. Cooling of an IGBT drive system with vaporizable dielectric fluid (VDF). 24th IEEE SEMI-THERM symposium. [8] Jiang Y, Zheng Q, Dong P, Yao J, Zhang H, Gao J. Conjugate heat transfer analysis of leading edge and downstream mist-air film cooling on turbine vane. Int J Heat Mass Trans 2015;90:613-626. [9] Saw LH, Ye Y, Tay AAO. Electro-thermal analysis and integration issues of Lithium-ion battery for electric vehicles. Appl Energ 2014;97- 107. ng is a suitable thermal management solution for Li-ion battery with considering the cooling effect and cost. Thermal performance of the mist cooling with a constant mass flow rate of 0.37 gs-1 of mist is illustrated in Fig. 5. About 2 ms-1 of dry air and 0.37 gs-1 of mist is sufficient to maintain the dummy battery surface temperature below 40 oC. There is about 2.2 oC difference in the simulation and experimental results for 1 ms-1 of dry air flow. As shown in Fig. 5, experimental result for air velocity of 3 ms-1 is lower than the simulation result. This is due to the accumulation of mist to form a large droplet and condensate on the surface of the dummy battery. Eventually this will cause lower temperature recorded on the dummy battery surface and this phenomenon is not modelled in the simulation. The variation of the maximum and minimum temperature of the dummy battery is about 2.3 oC. Correlation of Nu with Re for mist cooling can be expressed as in equation below. 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 Nu = 0.8368Re0.5012 (2) Fig. 5. Change of dummy battery surface temperature with different velocity of mist. 4. Conclusions Computational fluid dynamics analysis was used to analyses the cooling performance of the dummy battery under forced air convection and mist cooling. Experimental work using ultrasonic mist generator to supply a mist into the air stream was conducted to validate the simulation results. Simulation results showed that 3 ms-1 of dry air is sufficient to maintain the dummy battery temperature well below 40 oC. On the other hand, when mist is added into the air stream to enhance the poor heat transfer performance of dry air, It is observed that by adding 0.37 gs-1 of mist into 2 ms-1 of cooling air is adequate to reduce the dummy battery surface temperature below 40 oC. The coolant is evaporated by absorbing a significant amount of heat from the heated surface and provide a cooling effect. Through injection of mist into the dry air stream, heat transfer performance of the cooling air can be extended further to the downstream. Through this study, it is concluded that mist cooling could provide an alternative cooling solution for Li-ion battery pack. In future study, mist cooling on a battery module will be investigated numerically and experimentally to assess the effect of mist cooling for a battery pack using different droplet size. Acknowledgements This work was supported by UTARRF Grant No. IPSR/RMC/UTARRF/2016-C1/B2 from UTAR and Science fund Grant No. 03-02-11-SF2016 from Ministry of Science, Technology and innovation, Malaysia. References [1] Rao Z, Wang S. A review of power battery thermal energy management. Rew Sust Energ Rev 2011;15:4554-4571. [2] Leng F, Tan, CM, Pecht M. Effect of temperature on the aging rate of Li-ion battery operating above room temperature. Sci. Rep 2015;5:12967. [3] Saw LH, Tay AAO, Zhang WL. Thermal management of Lithium-ion battery pack with liquid cooling. 31st SEMI-THERM 2015;298-302. [4] Saw LH, Ye Y, Tay AAO, Chong WT, Kuan SH, Yew MC. Computational fluid dynamic and thermal analysis of Lithium-ion battery pack with air cooling. Appl Energ 2016;177:783-792. [5] Jarrett A, Kim IY. Influence of operating conditions on the optimum design of electric vehicle battery cooling plates. J Pow Sour 2014;245:644- 655. [6] Somasundaram K, Birgersson E, Mujumdar AS. Thermal-electrochemical model for passive thermal management of a spiral-wound lithium- ion battery. J Pow Sour 2012;203:84-96. [7] Howes JC, Levett DB, Wilson ST, Marsala J, Saums DL. Cooling of an IGBT drive system with vaporizable dielectric fluid (VDF). 24th IEEE SEMI-THERM symposium. [8] Jiang Y, Zheng Q, Dong P, Yao J, Zhang H, Gao J. Conjugate heat transfer ana

head.ce:title: Feasibility study of mist cooling for lithium-ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lee Kong Chian Faculty of Engineering and Science, UTAR, Kajang, 43000, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Lee Kong Chian Faculty of Engineering and Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Fauclty of Engineering, University of Malaya, Kuala Lumpur, 50603, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mechanical Engineering Education, Universitas Negeri Sebelas Maret, Jl. Ir. Sutami 36A, Surakarta 57126, Indonesia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Li-ion battery is widely used as an energy storage system in electric vehicles and it has several advantages such as long cycle life, high energy density, superior usable capacity, low self-discharge rate and fast charging capability. However, the optimum operating temperature window of the battery is limited to 25 °C - 40 °C. Actual performance can deviate substantially if it is operated at higher or lower temperatures. Although electron and ion mobility is increased at high temperature due to low internal cell resistance, It may initiate irreversible chemical reactions which can cause thermal runaway. At temperature below 0 °C, the cell’s internal resistance is high and the performance starts to deteriorate due to Lithium plating. Therefore, this highlights the importance of the thermal management system for the Li-ion battery. In this study, thermal performance of the mist cooling was studied and compared with conventional dry air cooling using computational fluid dynamic simulation. Simulation results are then validated with experimental data and the correlation between Nu and Re was derived. From this study, it is shown that mist flow rate of 0.37 gs-1 and air velocity of 2 ms-1 is sufficient to maintain the surface temperature of dummy battery below 40 °C. Hence, mist cooling can be used as a new cooling solution for the Li-ion battery.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of power battery thermal energy management
Effect of temperature on the aging rate of Li-ion battery operating above room temperature

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Saw LH, Tay AAO, Zhang WL. Thermal management of Lithium-ion battery pack with liquid cooling. 31st SEMI-THERM 2015;298-302.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Computational fluid dynamic and thermal analysis of Lithium-ion battery pack with air cooling
Influence of operating conditions on the optimum design of electric vehicle battery cooling plates
Thermal-electrochemical model for passive thermal management of a spiral-wound lithium-ion battery

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Howes JC, Levett DB, Wilson ST, Marsala J, Saums DL. Cooling of an IGBT drive system with vaporizable dielectric fluid (VDF). 24th IEEE SEMI-THERM symposium.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conjugate heat transfer analysis of leading edge and downstream mist-air film cooling on turbine vane

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Saw LH, Ye Y, Tay AAO. Electro-thermal analysis and integration issues of Lithium-ion battery for electric vehicles. Appl Energ 2014;97-107.
